# Tier 1 Practice Problems {.unnumbered}

## Overview

Tier 1 problems are practice and will make up the bulk of your homework. They vary in type — some are conceptual ("why is there a negative sign in Fourier's law?"), some are quantitative ("find the heat flux through this composite wall"), and some are analytical ("derive the fin efficiency for these boundary conditions").

They don't require a full technical report, but a neat solution with a clearly stated answer, with units, and probably a bit of narration ("Melting in about 30 minutes indeed seems realistic") is necessary.


## Why These Problems Exist

The best way to learn a topic is to struggle - you cannot read someone else's solution to learn a topic. (And ChatGPT, etc., fall into the category of "someone else".) You were not faced with uncertainty, you did not have to review your notes, you did not have to make choices about what path to take, you didn't have to be careful with the units, and you didn't have to check your work. In short, you have to do problems to learn the material.

These Tier 1 problems are opportunities to work through the material so that you can actually learn. They are worth 10% of your grade and graded primarily on completion. That is not because they are unimportant — it is because the point is the doing, not the product. 

## Submission Format

Each Tier 1 problem follows the same two-stage process as the technical reports:

**Stage 1 (Friday, 6:00 AM):** Submit a genuine attempt. Legible work, units carried, answer stated. Completion probably earns full credit for this stage. Submitting Stage 1 unlocks the Stage 2 assignment, which includes milestone checkpoints.

**Stage 2 (Monday, 6:00 PM):** Submit a clean final version. This is lightly graded on correctness, and .

## What a Good Tier 1 Submission Looks Like

- Work is legible and organized
- Units are carried through every line
- Answer is clearly stated
- One sentence explains the physical meaning of the result: not just "Q = 245 W" but "Q = 245 W, which is consistent with the expected direction of heat flow from hot to cold"

That last sentence is often the most valuable part. It is also the hardest to write without understanding the problem — which is precisely the point.

## Grading

| Stage | Weight | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Stage 1 (initial) | Completion | Genuine attempt submitted on time |
| Stage 2 (final) | Completion + light correctness | Clean solution, units, answer, physical interpretation |

TAs will return these quickly. Feedback will be brief — a flag if something is physically wrong, otherwise a check.
